flashcards.back: Memory management technique creating illusion of large memory by using disk storage, allowing execution of processes not entirely in RAM.
Pages are loaded into memory only when needed (on page fault), reducing initial memory load and I/O overhead.
When a process accesses a page marked as invalid (not in physical memory), triggering OS page handler.
Allows parent/child processes to share pages initially. Copies page only when either modifies it, optimizing fork() operations.
Theoretical algorithm replacing page that won't be used for longest future time. Used as benchmark.

flashcards.front: Belady's Anomaly occurs in which algorithm?
flashcards.back: FIFO page replacement. Increasing frames can paradoxically increase page faults in some access patterns.
Determines set of pages process is actively using to prevent thrashing by ensuring adequate frame allocation.
Hardware cache storing recent page table entries to speed up virtual-to-physical address translation.
EAT = (1-p)*memory_access + p*(page_fault_overhead + swap_out + swap_in). p = page fault rate.
LRU removes least recently used page. LFU removes least frequently used page. LRU more common in practice.
Severe performance degradation when system spends more time handling page faults than executing processes.
Global selects from all frames (better utilization). Local selects only from process's frames (fair allocation).
Indicates if page has been modified. Unmodified pages can be discarded; modified pages require writeback.
FIFO variant: Pages with reference bit=0 are replaced. Pages with bit=1 get second chance (bit cleared).
Contains one entry per physical frame (vs per virtual page). Reduces memory overhead but increases lookup time.
Page: Fixed-size virtual memory block. Frame: Fixed-size physical memory block that holds a page.
Set of pages currently loaded in physical memory for a particular process.
Indicates whether page is in memory (valid) or requires loading from disk (invalid).
1. Trap to OS 2. Check page validity 3. Find free frame 4. Load page 5. Update tables 6. Restart instruction
Combines logical segmentation (for programmer view) with physical paging (for memory management efficiency).
Fixed-size allocation units (pages/frames). Unused space within allocated unit that can't be utilized.
Files treated as virtual memory segments. File I/O performed through memory accesses via page faults.

flashcards.front: Role of MMU in virtual memory
flashcards.back: Translates virtual addresses to physical using page tables. Handles TLB lookups and page fault triggers.
Loading pages before they're needed (opposite of demand paging). Reduces initial page faults at process start.
